origin.solution: For a dust fluid element that marginally escapes to spatial infinity, it arrives at infinity with zero local kinetic energy relative to a stationary observer. A stationary observer at spatial infinity has a 4-velocity proportional to the timelike Killing vector $\xi^{\mu}$. Given the normalization condition $\xi^{\mu} \xi_{\mu} \to -1$ at infinity, the 4-velocity of such a stationary observer asymptotically aligns exactly with the Killing vector, i.e., $u^{\mu}_{\text{obs}} \to \xi^{\mu}$. Since the escaping particle has zero local velocity at infinity, its 4-velocity must also asymptotically align with the observer's 4-velocity, meaning $u^{\mu} \to \xi^{\mu}$ at spatial infinity. Evaluating the conserved quantity $E_{\xi}$ at spatial infinity yields: $E_{\xi} = -\xi_{\mu} u^{\mu} \to -\xi_{\mu} \xi^{\mu} = -(\xi^{\mu} \xi_{\mu}) = -(-1) = 1$. By the conservation of $E_{\xi}$ along the dust flow lines, this value remains constant throughout the entire trajectory from any finite radius to infinity. Therefore, the critical value of $E_{\xi}$ for marginal escape is exactly 1. If $E_{\xi} > 1$, the particle possesses residual kinetic energy at infinity and escapes unbound; if $E_{\xi} < 1$, it cannot reach infinity and remains in a bound orbit.
